critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed — chronological split, group-aware ranking, time-safe feature generation, and an honest scope statement. My analysis surfaces three substantive issues, one of which is severe enough to undermine validity.

**Issue 1: The 7-day unresolved drop creates survivorship bias in the label distribution that is not uniform across the train/validation/test windows.**

The claim: dropping unresolved cases after 7 days produces stable supervision without distorting the label distribution.

Why it might be wrong: the proportion of unresolved cases at the 7-day cutoff is not constant over time. During attacker campaigns, novel threat types, or SOC staffing shortfalls, resolution latency increases. This means the train set loses a larger fraction of its most ambiguous or novel cases precisely when those cases are most informative. Worse, the cases that survive into the labeled set are systematically those the SOC resolved quickly — i.e., alerts matching known patterns. The model is therefore trained on a biased sample that overrepresents recognizable, fast-resolved incidents. In the test window, particularly if it sits 15–18 months into the data (plausibly after a threat landscape shift), the drop rate may differ, changing the effective label prior and the difficulty of the ranking problem. If the test window has a higher drop rate than the train window, NDCG@10 is computed over a harder residual set, making the model look worse. If lower, it looks better. Neither is interpretable.

Evidence one way or the other: compare the fraction of alerts dropped per month across the 18-month window. If the drop fraction is stable (±2–3 percentage points), the bias is likely tolerable. If it varies with incident volume or SOC headcount events, the training distribution is materially distorted. A supplementary analysis fitting the model on retained-only vs. all-available alerts using NDCG computed only on the retained subset would quantify this.

**Issue 2: The baseline is underspecified in a way that conflates two independent hypotheses, and the stronger confound is not isolated.**

The claim: the gradient-boosted classifier baseline isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information sources simultaneously, making LambdaMART the correct comparison.

Why it might be wrong: the methodology says the baseline uses 'only structured metadata' while LambdaMART uses structured metadata plus entity recurrence statistics, asset/user context, MITRE mappings, sparse text features, and historical analyst feedback summaries. These are two simultaneous differences: (a) ranking objective vs. classification objective, and (b) richer feature set vs. metadata-only. If LambdaMART wins, you cannot attribute the gain to the ranking formulation. The description acknowledges this only implicitly ('isolates both'), which is actually the opposite of isolation — it bundles both. A gradient-boosted classifier trained on the full LambdaMART feature set and ranked by predicted probability within groups is the missing middle baseline. Without it, the experiment cannot distinguish 'ranking formulation matters' from 'extra features matter.' This is not a minor gap: in practice, entity recurrence statistics and historical analyst feedback summaries are high-signal features that alone could account for most of the NDCG lift.

Evidence one way or the other: train the classifier baseline with the full feature set. If NDCG on the full-feature classifier approaches LambdaMART, the ranking objective is not the driver. If LambdaMART still wins, the formulation is doing real work. The ablation by feature family partially addresses this but only within the LambdaMART model, not across model classes.

**Issue 3: Label-policy confound — the graded relevance target encodes SOC escalation policy, not ground truth severity, and this conflation is asymmetric across business units.**

The claim: downstream operational consequence (confirmed incident severity, containment/remediation within 7 days) is a stable proxy for true alert priority.

Why it might be wrong: the label is a function of (a) actual threat severity and (b) SOC escalation policy, analyst bandwidth, and tooling at the time of the alert. Business units with faster escalation pipelines or more mature playbooks will have more label-2 events in the training data, not because their alerts are more severe but because their resolution process is faster. The model will learn to rank alerts from high-bandwidth business units higher. The subgroup check by business unit will detect this as a performance difference, but it won't distinguish 'the model is miscalibrated across BUs' from 'the model correctly learned BU-specific threat profiles.' More concretely, if a business unit was under-resourced during the training window and over-resourced during the test window (or vice versa), the label distribution for that BU shifts, and NDCG on that subgroup is not comparable across windows.

This is partially acknowledged in the scope statement ('policy-driven label bias'), but the methodology proposes no mitigation and no diagnostic. The subgroup check by BU is necessary but not sufficient — it will show variance without explaining it.

Evidence one way or the other: compute label-2 rate per business unit per quarter and test whether it correlates with analyst headcount or ticket-closure-time metrics. If it does, the labels are partially encoding resource availability. A sensitivity analysis using only business units with stable label rates across the 18-month window would bound the magnitude of this confound.

**Summary:** Issue 2 is the most structurally damaging — the experiment as designed cannot attribute gains to the ranking formulation vs. the feature set, which is the central hypothesis. Issue 1 introduces a time-varying sample selection bias that may systematically inflate or deflate test performance. Issue 3 is acknowledged but unaddressed. The methodology is operationally sophisticated but requires a full-feature classifier baseline and a drop-rate stability check to be interpretable.
all_issues_raised: Dropping unresolved 7-day cases creates a time-varying survivorship bias that distorts the label distribution non-uniformly across train, validation, and test windows.
The single baseline simultaneously conflates ranking objective and feature set differences, making it impossible to attribute NDCG gains to the ranking formulation vs. the richer feature set.

The graded relevance target encodes SOC escalation policy and analyst bandwidth, not just threat severity, creating a label-policy confound that is asymmetric across business units and unmitigated in the design.

The 7-day resolution window systematically drops the hardest high-severity cases (APT, ransomware) and creates right-censoring at the test window boundary, biasing the label distribution and inflating apparent performance.
The single baseline simultaneously confounds the ranking-formulation hypothesis and the richer-feature-set hypothesis; two additional ablation conditions are required to disentangle them.

Prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations may cause the model to memorize historical severity patterns for known combinations rather than generalize, which the unseen-rule-ID subgroup check does not fully test.
Label construction reflects prior analyst triage policy through incident record linkage, creating survivorship bias that causes the model to reproduce rather than improve the existing prioritization.

Dropping unresolved cases after 7 days removes structurally long-dwell alert types (lateral movement, persistence) and creates spurious correlation with analyst load periods, biasing training away from the highest-value alerts.

NDCG@10 on variable-size queue-day groups may be dominated by small-group query days where the metric is trivially achievable, inflating the headline result relative to high-volume operational conditions.

The baseline conflates two factors (ranking objective and feature set) by using a pointwise-loss classifier rather than a ranking-objective model on the same structured features, preventing clean attribution of gains.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
Overall this is a well-constructed experimental design. The chronological split, group-aware ranking, time-safe feature generation, and explicit exclusion of outcome-leaking fields are all correct. The scope limitations stated at the end are honest. I looked hard for fundamental flaws and found a small number of genuine issues worth naming, plus one that is borderline.

**Issue 1: The 7-day resolution window creates a structurally biased label distribution that interacts badly with the chronological split.**

Claim: Dropping unresolved-after-7-days cases removes only ambiguous labels and leaves a clean supervised signal.

Mechanism of failure: Cases dropped for non-resolution are not missing at random. Complex, novel, or stealthy intrusions — exactly the high-priority cases the system should rank highly — are disproportionately likely to exceed a 7-day resolution window. Ransomware, APT lateral movement, and supply-chain compromises routinely require weeks of investigation. These cases will be dropped from training and evaluation, systematically under-representing the highest-stakes tail of the severity distribution. Separately, near the end of each window (especially the test window boundary), cases opened in the final 7 days have not had time to resolve, creating a right-censoring artifact. If the test window is a hard calendar boundary, a disproportionate share of test-set cases opened in the last week will be dropped or mislabeled, shrinking the effective test set and potentially biasing NDCG@10 upward if dropped cases would have been hard positives.

Evidence: Compare the resolution-rate distribution stratified by eventual incident severity. If High/Critical cases resolve within 7 days at a significantly lower rate than Medium cases, the label scheme is systematically discarding the most important positive examples. Also compare alert volume in the last 7 days of the test window before and after the drop, and check whether NDCG@10 is sensitive to the choice of 7 vs. 14 vs. 21 days.

**Issue 2: The baseline comparison does not isolate the ranking formulation from the added information source as claimed.**

Claim: 'This baseline is the correct comparison for the hypothesis because it isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source from a realistic metadata-only scoring system.'

Mechanism of failure: The proposed model differs from the baseline in two ways simultaneously: (a) learning objective (LambdaMART ranking loss vs. pointwise classification loss) and (b) feature set (adds text features, entity recurrence statistics, prior-pattern summaries). The prose acknowledges this but frames it as 'isolating both,' which is subtly wrong — you cannot simultaneously isolate two variables with one comparison. If LambdaMART outperforms the baseline, you cannot attribute the gain to either the ranking formulation or the richer feature set without a third condition: a gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same full feature set and then ranked by predicted probability. Similarly, a LambdaMART model trained on metadata-only features would isolate the ranking loss contribution. Without these two additional ablations, the main result is confounded and the hypothesis about the ranking formulation specifically is untested. The ablation-by-feature-family section partially addresses this but only if those ablations are run within the LambdaMART framework, not as a cross-objective comparison.

Evidence: Add (1) GBDT classifier with full feature set ranked by probability, and (2) LambdaMART with metadata-only features. If the gains from (1) vs. baseline match the gains from full LambdaMART vs. baseline, the ranking formulation is not the driver. If (2) vs. baseline shows the same gap, the features are not the driver either.

**Issue 3: The 'prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations' are a potential label leakage vector that is not fully neutralized by the stated exclusions.**

Claim: Historical analyst feedback included only as prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations is safe from leakage.

Mechanism of failure: Prior-pattern summaries aggregated over rule-asset-user combinations are computed from historical disposition outcomes. If those summaries are computed over the entire training window without further chronological restriction at the per-alert level, a summary computed as of alert time T could include outcomes of earlier alerts in the same queue-day group that have already been resolved. More importantly, for recurring rule-asset-user tuples, the summary statistic is a strong proxy for the alert's own eventual label — not because the current alert's outcome is included, but because the combination is reliably high- or low-severity. This is not classic leakage, but it means the model may be primarily learning 'this rule-asset-user combination always fires on high-severity incidents' rather than 'this specific alert, given its context, is high priority.' That is potentially useful in production, but it conflates memorization of historical patterns with generalization to novel attacker behavior, and it creates optimistic evaluation on stable rule-asset-user combinations while hiding poor performance on new combinations. The subgroup check on unseen rule IDs partially tests this, but unseen rule IDs is a weak proxy — a new rule on a known asset class will not appear in that subgroup.

Evidence: Run the subgroup analysis stratified by (a) rule-asset-user combinations seen ≥N times in training vs. <N times, and (b) alert families where prior-pattern summaries have high vs. low coverage. If NDCG@10 degrades sharply in the low-coverage stratum, the model is memorizing summaries rather than generalizing.

**Borderline issue: The queue-day grouping assumption.**

Grouping by analyst queue-day and business unit is sensible but implicitly assumes that the ranking competition is confined within these groups. In practice, analysts may triage across queues, and the model's ranking within a queue-day group may not reflect the actual competition for analyst attention. This is a deployment assumption worth flagging but is not a flaw in the experimental design per se — it is a known scope limitation. I mention it only because if queue membership itself shifts (e.g., routing rules change during the 18-month window), the group definitions applied retrospectively may not match the live groups, creating noise in the evaluation. This is addressable by verifying routing rule stability, not a fundamental problem.

**Summary:** Two genuine methodological issues: the 7-day censoring window creates structured bias against the highest-severity cases and creates a right-censoring artifact at the test boundary; and the baseline comparison does not isolate the two dimensions of the hypothesis, leaving the result confounded. The third issue (prior-pattern summaries) is real but partially mitigated by the stated ablations. The design is otherwise strong.

issues_raised: Label construction reflects prior analyst triage policy through incident record linkage, creating survivorship bias that causes the model to reproduce rather than improve the existing prioritization.

Dropping unresolved cases after 7 days removes structurally long-dwell alert types (lateral movement, persistence) and creates spurious correlation with analyst load periods, biasing training away from the highest-value alerts.

NDCG@10 on variable-size queue-day groups may be dominated by small-group query days where the metric is trivially achievable, inflating the headline result relative to high-volume operational conditions.

The baseline conflates two factors (ranking objective and feature set) by using a pointwise-loss classifier rather than a ranking-objective model on the same structured features, preventing clean attribution of gains.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured methodology overall. Let me work through the genuine concerns I can identify, organized by root cause.

**Root Cause 1: Label Construction Creates a Survivorship and Policy-Reflection Problem**

The graded relevance scheme (2/1/0) is anchored to downstream incident records. The claim is that these labels reflect 'operational consequence.' The mechanism of failure: incident records are themselves the output of analyst triage decisions. If an alert was deprioritized in the queue (by the current system or human habit), it was less likely to be investigated, which means it was less likely to become a confirmed incident, which means it receives label 0. The training signal therefore partly encodes 'alerts the prior system ranked highly got investigated and confirmed.' LambdaMART then learns to reproduce that prior ordering. The ablation on historical analyst feedback summaries partially addresses this, but the label construction itself is the issue — not the features. This is not fully mitigated by excluding queue-order signals as features, because the label *is* already contaminated by queue-order effects upstream.

What would constitute evidence: Check the correlation between prior-system ranking position and label assignment. If label=2 alerts cluster heavily in the top deciles of prior queue position, the labels are reflecting prior policy rather than ground truth operational consequence. A comparison against a random subset of low-queue-ranked alerts that were independently re-investigated (e.g., by threat hunting) would be the cleanest test.

**Root Cause 2: Dropped Unresolved Cases Introduce Selection Bias That Interacts with Alert Volume and Incident Duration**

The methodology drops cases unresolved after 7 days. The claim is that this gives 'stable supervision.' The failure mechanism: some alert families — especially lateral movement, persistence, and slow-burn exfiltration — are structurally more likely to remain unresolved at day 7. Dropping them removes exactly the alert types most important to surface early. The resulting training distribution underrepresents long-dwell incidents, which biases the model toward quick-confirm alert types (endpoint alerts tied to commodity malware, for instance). Additionally, during periods of high analyst load (incident surges, staffing gaps), more cases will be unresolved at day 7 simply because analysts are overwhelmed — creating a spurious correlation between queue congestion and label dropout rate. The model may then implicitly learn that certain time windows are low-signal, compounding the policy-reflection issue.

What would constitute evidence: Report the label-dropout rate by alert family, MITRE tactic category, and calendar month. If lateral movement or persistence tactics have materially higher dropout rates than execution or exfiltration, the training set is biased against the highest-value alerts. Sensitivity analysis: vary the cutoff from 5 to 14 days and observe how label distribution and NDCG@10 change.

**Root Cause 3: Grouped Ranking Within Queue-Day Conflates Group Size Variance with Model Quality**

NDCG@10 as the primary metric on groups defined by analyst queue-day and business unit is stated as approximating a real triage slate. The failure mechanism: queue-day groups will have highly variable sizes. On low-volume days or small business units, a group may contain fewer than 10 alerts, making NDCG@10 ill-defined or trivially achievable (if a group has 3 alerts, getting any relevant one ranked first maximizes NDCG@10 regardless of discrimination quality). On high-volume days, NDCG@10 captures only the top slice of a much larger decision problem. If the model improves NDCG@10 predominantly by performing well on small-group days (which are easier and more numerous), the metric gain may not reflect improved prioritization on the high-stakes, high-volume days that matter most operationally.

What would constitute evidence: Report NDCG@10 stratified by group size quartile. If gains are concentrated in small-group (<10 alert) queries, the headline metric is inflated relative to operational value. Also verify that the bootstrap intervals are computed over queue-days rather than over individual alerts — pooling alerts would understate variance substantially.

**Root Cause 4: The Baseline Is Misspecified in a Way That Could Flatter LambdaMART**

The baseline is described as a gradient-boosted classifier ranked by predicted probability within queue groups. The claim is this 'isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source.' This is true as stated, but the baseline is underspecified: a gradient-boosted classifier optimizing log-loss or cross-entropy on a three-class or binary label is not the correct analog to LambdaMART. Classifiers optimizing pointwise losses frequently produce well-calibrated probabilities but suboptimal rankings. A more demanding baseline would be a gradient-boosted model trained with a pairwise or listwise ranking objective on the same structured-only feature set. Without this, part of the observed NDCG gain may reflect the ranking objective formulation rather than the additional features (entity recurrence, text, MITRE mappings). The methodology claims the comparison isolates 'both the ranking formulation and the added information source,' but that means neither is cleanly isolated.

What would constitute evidence: Add a third condition — LambdaMART trained on structured metadata only (same features as the baseline, ranking objective). This creates a 2x2 design: {classifier, ranker} x {metadata-only, full feature set}. Without this cell, the contribution of the ranking objective versus the new features cannot be separated.

**Assessment of Non-Issues**

The chronological split is correctly implemented and the stated time-safety constraints on feature generation are sound. Excluding final disposition fields and closure codes is the right call. The subgroup checks on unseen rule IDs are valuable and address a real generalization concern. The caveat about not proving causal dwell-time reduction is appropriately scoped. These design elements are genuinely well-considered.

**Summary**

Three issues are genuine methodological concerns: (1) label contamination by prior policy through the incident record linkage, (2) selection bias from dropping unresolved cases that disproportionately removes high-value alert types, and (3) the baseline misspecification that confounds ranking objective and feature set contributions. Issue (3) is the most fixable; issues (1) and (2) are structural and require empirical characterization rather than redesign.
